message.content:
User Execution involves adversaries leveraging user actions to execute malicious code. This often occurs through social engineering tactics, such as phishing, where users are tricked into opening malicious documents or links. While commonly seen during the Initial Access phase, it can also happen later, such as when adversaries place files in shared directories or on desktops, hoping users will execute them. Techniques include enabling remote access software, downloading malware, or falling for tech support scams facilitated through phishing, vishing, or spoofed call centers. Notable groups like LAPSUS$ have even recruited insiders to provide credentials or install remote management software, granting adversaries control over authenticated systems.

Potential impacts include unauthorized system access, data exfiltration, and further network compromise. Mitigation strategies involve user education on recognizing phishing attempts, implementing robust email filtering, restricting macro execution in documents, and employing multi-factor authentication (MFA) to reduce the risk of credential misuse. Regular security training and awareness programs can significantly reduce the likelihood of successful user execution attacks.
